title: Listening - Part 4: Caring for Injured Wildlife
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Eva, who manages a wildlife rehabilitation centre. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: This afternoon I’m speaking to Eva Price, who manages a wildlife rehabilitation centre. Eva, what led you to this work? || Eva: When I was a teenager, I found a hedgehog beside a road and brought it home, thinking food and warmth would solve everything. It needed specialist care, so I contacted a local centre. [24]Seeing the trained staff treat that injured hedgehog made me realise wildlife care required real knowledge, and I began volunteering there[/24]. I later studied animal care and eventually became manager. || Presenter: What mistakes do members of the public make with injured animals? || Eva: Most people genuinely want to help. The problem is that a wild animal is not a pet. [25]People sometimes feed an injured animal unsuitable food or handle it repeatedly because they believe this will comfort it[/25]. Usually it is better to contact experts and keep the animal quiet until help is available. || Presenter: How do you decide that an animal is ready to return to the wild? || Eva: Healing a wound isn’t enough. The animal must behave naturally again. [26]Before release, it must be able to find food for itself and avoid people rather than looking to staff for attention[/26]. An animal that becomes dependent on humans may not survive outdoors, however healthy it looks. || Presenter: You have many volunteers. What jobs do they do? || Eva: New volunteers often imagine they will spend their day holding young animals. In reality, direct contact is kept to a minimum. [27]One of their most valuable tasks is carefully recording weight, feeding and behaviour, because those details help us judge recovery[/27]. Cleaning equipment is also essential, even if it is less glamorous. || Presenter: How is the centre funded? || Eva: Donations are vital, but they vary from month to month. We also visit schools and give talks at community events. [28]Our educational visits help raise money, while also teaching people how to protect wild animals before they become injured[/28]. Preventing problems is always better than treating them. || Presenter: What is the hardest part of your job? || Eva: Releasing a recovered animal is wonderful. The hardest moments are different. [29]Sometimes an animal arrives so badly injured that treatment cannot save it, and accepting that is emotionally very difficult[/29]. We support one another because nobody becomes completely used to that part of the work. || Presenter: When do you feel most satisfied? || Eva: People think it must be when an animal comes towards me affectionately. Actually, the opposite is true. [30]When an animal that once needed us runs away immediately at release, I know it is behaving as a wild animal should[/30]. That is the success we aim for. || Presenter: Eva, thank you. || Eva: Thank you for inviting me.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first encouraged Eva to volunteer at a wildlife centre?

options.A: She wanted to study for a qualification in animal care.
options.B: She was impressed by expert care of an injured animal.
options.C: She needed advice about keeping a hedgehog as a pet.

questions.explanation: Eva saw professional staff treat an injured hedgehog and realised she wanted to help.

questions.question: What mistake does Eva say people sometimes make?

options.A: They try to care for wild animals in inappropriate ways.
options.B: They wait too long before moving animals from roads.
options.C: They expect wildlife centres to collect every animal.

questions.explanation: She says people may provide the wrong food or handle injured animals too often.

questions.question: What must an animal do before it can be released?

options.A: Stay calm when it is examined by staff.
options.B: Live with similar animals for several weeks.
options.C: Find food independently and avoid humans.

questions.explanation: Eva says the animal must feed itself and stop seeking attention from staff.

questions.question: What does Eva value about volunteers' work?

options.A: They note information that supports treatment decisions.
options.B: They provide young animals with human contact.
options.C: They answer emergency calls from the public.

questions.explanation: Volunteer records of weight, feeding and behaviour help the centre judge recovery.

questions.question: What is one purpose of the centre's educational visits?

options.A: To find families able to care for animals.
options.B: To obtain support and prevent future harm.
options.C: To provide training for new members of staff.

questions.explanation: School and community visits both raise funds and teach people how to protect wildlife.

questions.question: Which aspect of her job does Eva find most difficult?

options.A: Knowing that some injured animals cannot recover.
options.B: Explaining her decisions to members of the public.
options.C: Finding space for all the animals brought in.

questions.explanation: She finds it very painful when an animal is too seriously injured to be saved.

questions.question: What gives Eva the greatest sense of achievement?

options.A: An animal returning to visit the centre.
options.B: An animal showing confidence around people.
options.C: An animal behaving independently when released.

questions.explanation: She regards an animal running away at release as proof it has returned to natural behaviour.
